categories.title: General Plug-In Modules
categories.description: Self-contained modules (30–45 min each) that can be swapped into the main program based on audience needs.

modules.title: Cloud Migration & Multi-Cloud Strategy
modules.purpose: Most enterprise products today involve a cloud component—whether migrating from on-premise, adopting cloud-native, or managing multi-cloud complexity. This module provides frameworks for making cloud strategy decisions and executing migrations safely.
modules.bestFor: Organizations moving from on-premise to cloud or managing multi-cloud environments
learningObjectives: Evaluate cloud deployment models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, hybrid)
Apply the 6 Rs of cloud migration to your application portfolio
Design multi-cloud strategies that avoid vendor lock-in
Plan cloud migrations with minimal disruption to operations

keyTopics.title: The 6 Rs of Cloud Migration
keyTopics.description: Rehost (lift-and-shift), Replatform (lift-and-optimize), Refactor (re-architect for cloud-native), Repurchase (switch to SaaS), Retire (decommission), Retain (keep on-premise). Each application in your portfolio may follow a different R.

keyTopics.title: Multi-Cloud Strategy
keyTopics.description: Best-of-breed vs. single-provider. Avoiding lock-in through abstraction layers and portable architectures. Cost optimization through workload placement. The operational complexity tax of multi-cloud.

keyTopics.title: Migration Execution
keyTopics.description: Application dependency mapping, migration wave planning, cutover strategies, rollback procedures, and post-migration validation. The importance of landing zones and governance guardrails.

modules.title: Legacy Modernization & Strangler Fig Pattern
modules.purpose: Most enterprises don't build greenfield—they modernize legacy systems while keeping the business running. This module covers proven patterns for incremental modernization without big-bang risk.
modules.bestFor: Teams maintaining legacy systems while building modern replacements
learningObjectives: Assess legacy systems for modernization readiness
Apply the Strangler Fig pattern for incremental modernization
Manage the dual operating model during transition
Avoid common modernization anti-patterns

keyTopics.title: Legacy Assessment Framework
keyTopics.description: Evaluate legacy systems on business value vs. technical health. High value + poor health = modernize. Low value + poor health = retire. High value + good health = maintain. Low value + good health = candidate for replacement with SaaS.

keyTopics.title: The Strangler Fig Pattern
keyTopics.description: Named after trees that gradually envelop their host. Route new functionality to modern system while legacy handles existing features. Gradually migrate features until legacy can be decommissioned. No big-bang cutover—risk is spread over time.

keyTopics.title: Data Migration Strategies
keyTopics.description: Dual-write, change data capture (CDC), ETL pipelines, and event sourcing as data migration approaches. Maintaining consistency during the transition period. Hardware: migrating from legacy industrial controllers to modern edge computing platforms.

modules.title: Regulatory Compliance Deep-Dive
modules.purpose: For organizations operating in regulated industries, compliance isn't optional—it shapes architecture, process, and vendor selection. This module dives deep into the most impactful regulatory frameworks.
modules.bestFor: Regulated industries: finance (SOX), healthcare (HIPAA), EU operations (GDPR), government
learningObjectives: Map regulatory requirements to technical architecture decisions
Design data handling practices for GDPR, HIPAA, and SOX compliance
Build audit-ready systems with continuous evidence collection
Navigate the intersection of multiple regulatory frameworks

keyTopics.title: GDPR Technical Requirements
keyTopics.description: Data protection by design and by default, data subject rights (access, erasure, portability), data processing agreements, cross-border data transfer mechanisms (adequacy decisions, SCCs), and breach notification within 72 hours.

keyTopics.title: SOX & Financial Controls
keyTopics.description: IT general controls (access management, change management, operations), automated controls within applications, audit trail requirements, and segregation of duties in system design.

keyTopics.title: HIPAA & Healthcare Data
keyTopics.description: Protected Health Information (PHI) handling, BAA (Business Associate Agreement) requirements, minimum necessary principle, encryption requirements at rest and in transit, and access logging requirements.

modules.title: AI/ML Product Integration
modules.purpose: Integrating AI/ML into enterprise products introduces unique lifecycle challenges: data pipelines, model training, evaluation, deployment, monitoring for drift, and ethical considerations. This module bridges ML engineering and product management.
modules.bestFor: Teams integrating AI/ML capabilities into enterprise products
learningObjectives: Understand the ML product lifecycle from data to deployment
Design MLOps pipelines for model training, evaluation, and deployment
Monitor models in production for performance drift
Navigate ethical and bias considerations in AI-powered products

keyTopics.title: The ML Product Lifecycle
keyTopics.description: Data collection and labeling → Feature engineering → Model training and evaluation → Deployment → Monitoring → Retraining. Each phase has unique quality gates, unlike traditional software. Models degrade over time as data distributions shift.

keyTopics.title: MLOps Pipeline Design
keyTopics.description: CI/CD for ML: version data and models (not just code), automate training pipelines, A/B test model versions, canary deploy new models, and monitor prediction quality. The challenge: reproducibility requires tracking data, code, hyperparameters, and environment.

keyTopics.title: AI Ethics & Bias
keyTopics.description: Bias in training data leads to biased predictions. Fairness metrics and bias testing as part of the ML quality gate. Explainability requirements for regulated industries. The human-in-the-loop principle for high-stakes decisions.

modules.title: Product Analytics & Data-Driven Decisions
modules.purpose: Building the product is only half the job—understanding how it's used and whether it delivers value requires systematic product analytics. This module bridges product management and engineering with data-driven practices.
modules.bestFor: Product teams seeking to improve data-driven product management
learningObjectives: Define and instrument key product metrics
Design analytics architectures for product insight
Apply experimentation (A/B testing) to product decisions
Build a data-driven product culture

keyTopics.title: Product Metrics Framework
keyTopics.description: North Star Metric (the one metric that best captures the value you deliver), HEART framework (Happiness, Engagement, Adoption, Retention, Task success), and pirate metrics (AARRR: Acquisition, Activation, Retention, Revenue, Referral).

keyTopics.title: Instrumentation & Analytics Architecture
keyTopics.description: Event tracking strategy, data pipeline design (collection → processing → storage → analysis), real-time vs. batch analytics, and data governance (privacy, consent, retention).

keyTopics.title: Experimentation & A/B Testing
keyTopics.description: Hypothesis-driven product development. A/B test design: sample size, statistical significance, guardrail metrics. Common pitfalls: peeking at results too early, testing too many variants, ignoring long-term effects.

modules.title: API Strategy & Platform Thinking
modules.purpose: Many enterprise products evolve into platforms that others build upon. This module covers the strategic shift from product to platform thinking, with API design as the enabling foundation.
modules.bestFor: Organizations building platforms, marketplaces, or extensible products
learningObjectives: Distinguish product thinking from platform thinking
Design APIs as strategic business assets
Build developer experience (DX) as a competitive advantage
Plan API governance and lifecycle management

keyTopics.title: Product to Platform Evolution
keyTopics.description: The platform shift: from building all capabilities yourself to enabling others to build on your foundation. Network effects, ecosystem dynamics, and the flywheel of platform value creation.

keyTopics.title: API as Business Asset
keyTopics.description: APIs as products with their own lifecycle: design, versioning, deprecation, sunsetting. Developer experience (DX): documentation, SDKs, sandbox environments, and developer portals.

keyTopics.title: API Governance
keyTopics.description: Design standards (naming conventions, pagination, error handling), rate limiting and monetization, breaking change policies, and API versioning strategies (URL path, header, content negotiation).

modules.title: Hardware-Software Co-Design
modules.purpose: For products that span the hardware-software boundary—IoT devices, embedded systems, edge computing—coordinating hardware and software development is a unique challenge. This module expands the hardware examples from the core program into a dedicated deep-dive.
modules.bestFor: Teams building products with hardware and software components; IoT and embedded systems
learningObjectives: Coordinate hardware and software development timelines
Design hardware abstraction layers for flexibility
Manage the hardware prototype-to-production transition
Navigate certification and compliance for physical products

keyTopics.title: Hardware-Software Timeline Alignment
keyTopics.description: Hardware has long lead times (weeks-months) while software sprints in days-weeks. Strategies: hardware abstraction layers, emulators, FPGA prototyping, and modular hardware design that accommodates software evolution.

keyTopics.title: Prototype to Production
keyTopics.description: The valley between working prototype and production hardware: Design for Manufacturing (DFM), Design for Test (DFT), component sourcing and supply chain, manufacturing yield optimization, and production line bring-up.

keyTopics.title: Certification & Compliance
keyTopics.description: Navigating FCC (electromagnetic emissions), CE (European conformity), UL (safety), RoHS (hazardous substances), and industry-specific certifications. Pre-compliance testing to avoid expensive re-spins.

categories.title: Product Management Track
categories.description: Designed for product managers and product owners navigating enterprise product delivery.

modules.title: Stakeholder Management for Product Managers
modules.purpose: Product managers sit at the intersection of business, technology, and user experience. This module builds the stakeholder management skills specific to the PM role in enterprise contexts.
modules.bestFor: Product managers navigating complex stakeholder landscapes
learningObjectives: Map and manage enterprise stakeholder landscapes
Navigate competing priorities between business units
Communicate product decisions effectively to diverse audiences
Build credibility as the voice of the customer

keyTopics.title: Enterprise Stakeholder Mapping for PMs
keyTopics.description: Identifying decision-makers, influencers, and blockers. Building on Module 3's RACI: how PMs establish authority without positional power.

keyTopics.title: Prioritization Under Pressure
keyTopics.description: When every stakeholder's request is 'critical': using impact-effort matrices, weighted scoring, and transparent prioritization frameworks to make defensible decisions.

modules.title: Product Roadmap Governance
modules.purpose: Enterprise product roadmaps are political artifacts as much as strategic ones. This module covers how to build, communicate, and defend a roadmap that balances all stakeholders.
modules.bestFor: Product managers responsible for strategic roadmaps
learningObjectives: Build outcome-based roadmaps rather than feature lists
Balance competing investment categories in the roadmap
Communicate roadmap changes without losing stakeholder trust
Align product roadmap with business strategy

keyTopics.title: Outcome-Based Roadmapping
keyTopics.description: Shift from feature lists (what we'll build) to outcome goals (what we'll achieve). Timeframes: Now (committed), Next (planned), Later (exploratory). Preserves strategic flexibility.

keyTopics.title: Roadmap Communication
keyTopics.description: Different versions for different audiences: executive (strategic themes), engineering (technical scope), customers (value delivery). Managing expectations when plans change.

categories.title: Engineering Track
categories.description: Designed for engineering leads and architects deepening their enterprise product delivery skills.

modules.title: Technical Debt as Strategic Investment
modules.purpose: Technical debt isn't inherently bad—it's a strategic tool when managed deliberately. This module reframes debt from 'mess we made' to 'investment decision we track.'
modules.bestFor: Engineering leads managing debt alongside feature delivery
learningObjectives: Classify technical debt by type and business impact
Communicate debt in business terms to stakeholders
Build a systematic debt management practice
Balance debt reduction with feature delivery

keyTopics.title: Technical Debt Taxonomy
keyTopics.description: Deliberate vs. accidental debt. Prudent vs. reckless debt. The debt quadrant: Deliberate/Prudent ('We know this shortcut and we'll fix it'), Deliberate/Reckless ('We don't have time for design'), Accidental/Prudent ('Now we know how we should have done it'), Accidental/Reckless ('What's layered architecture?').

keyTopics.title: Debt Communication Framework
keyTopics.description: Translating technical debt into business language: risk of outage, cost of delayed features, developer productivity impact, and security vulnerability exposure. Making the invisible visible.

modules.title: Production Excellence & SRE Practices
modules.purpose: Expands Module 5's operations content for engineering teams responsible for production systems. Deep-dive into SRE practices, error budgets, and toil reduction.
learningObjectives: Implement SRE practices within engineering teams
Define and manage error budgets
Identify and reduce operational toil
Build production excellence as a team discipline

keyTopics.title: Error Budgets
keyTopics.description: If your SLO is 99.95% availability, your error budget is 0.05%. Spend it on feature velocity (accepting some risk) or save it for stability. When the budget is exhausted, freeze features and focus on reliability.

keyTopics.title: Toil Reduction
keyTopics.description: Toil is repetitive, manual, automatable work that scales linearly with system growth. Google's SRE principle: no more than 50% of an SRE's time should be toil. Identify, measure, and systematically automate away toil.

categories.title: Operations & Procurement Track
categories.description: Designed for operations teams, IT procurement, and vendor management professionals.

modules.title: Vendor Relationship Management
modules.purpose: Vendor selection is just the beginning. This module covers the ongoing discipline of managing vendor relationships for sustained value delivery.
modules.bestFor: IT procurement and vendor management professionals
learningObjectives: Build effective ongoing vendor relationships
Conduct structured vendor performance reviews
Manage contract renewals and negotiations
Plan for vendor transitions and exit strategies

keyTopics.title: Vendor Performance Management
keyTopics.description: Quarterly business reviews (QBRs): structure, metrics, escalation. SLA compliance tracking and trend analysis. Building a vendor performance scorecard.

keyTopics.title: Exit Planning & Vendor Transitions
keyTopics.description: Every vendor relationship should have an exit plan before you need one. Data portability, knowledge transfer, transition timelines, and parallel running periods.

modules.title: IT Service Management & ITIL Integration
modules.purpose: Enterprise products don't exist in isolation—they operate within ITSM frameworks. This module bridges product delivery with operational service management.
modules.bestFor: Operations teams integrating new products into existing IT service management
learningObjectives: Integrate new products into existing ITSM frameworks
Design service catalogs and support models
Bridge DevOps and ITSM practices
Plan for operational readiness and handoff

keyTopics.title: ITIL and DevOps Coexistence
keyTopics.description: ITIL and DevOps aren't enemies—they address different concerns. ITIL provides governance and service orientation; DevOps provides speed and automation. Bridging the gap for enterprise teams.

keyTopics.title: Operational Readiness Handoff
keyTopics.description: The handoff from project team to operations team is where many products stumble. Knowledge transfer, runbook creation, training, and the 'hypercare' period after launch.
